Lyon was a major center of which industry during the 16th–19th centuries, contributing greatly to its wealth?
The Lumière brothers, inventors of cinema, were from Lyon. What is the name of the institute and museum dedicated to them in the city?
Which ancient Roman theater, still used for performances today, sits on Fourvière Hill in Lyon?
Lyon's famous festival 'Fête des Lumières' (Festival of Lights) takes place every year in which month?
options: June, to mark the summer solstice
September, to open the cultural season
December, around the feast of the Immaculate Conception

lyon-part-dieu.text: The fictional detective character Inspecteur Maigret, created by Belgian writer Georges Simenon, frequently visits Lyon. But which Lyon-born fictional detective was created by writer Fred Vargas?
What is 'quenelle de brochet', a dish strongly associated with Lyon?
options: A steamed dumpling made from pike fish
A type of fried potato cake
A pastry filled with praline cream

lyon-part-dieu.text: Lyon was founded by the Romans under what ancient Latin name?
The Musée des Confluences in Lyon opened in 2014. What subjects does it cover?
options: Sciences, societies, and the history of the universe and humanity
The history of silk and textiles in Europe

lyon-part-dieu.text: Which Lyon football club plays at the Groupama Stadium and has won the French league title a record number of consecutive times?
The Presqu'île (peninsula) is the central district of Lyon. Between which two rivers is it situated?
Which legendary Lyon-based resistance leader during WWII was betrayed and captured by the Gestapo in 1943?
Lyon is known for its traditional painted wall murals called 'trompe-l'œil'. What organization is responsible for many of these murals?
The Basilica of Notre-Dame de Fourvière dominates Lyon's skyline from atop Fourvière Hill. When was it built?
options: Late 19th century, after the Franco-Prussian War
Early 20th century, during the Belle Époque

lyon-part-dieu.text: What is 'praline rose' as associated with Lyon?
options: A pink sugar-coated almond used in pastries

lyon-part-dieu.text: The Roman emperor Claudius, born in Lyon, is famous for having expanded the Roman Empire into which territory?

marseille-st-charles.text: Marseille is France's oldest city. By which ancient civilization was it founded around 600 BC?
What is the name of the famous spicy fish stew that is considered the signature dish of Marseille?
The Château d'If, a former island prison near Marseille, was made famous by which novel?
options: The Count of Monte Cristo by Alexandre Dumas
Notre-Dame de Paris by Victor Hugo

marseille-st-charles.text: Notre-Dame de la Garde, nicknamed 'La Bonne Mère' by Marseillais, stands on a hill overlooking the city. What is at the very top of its bell tower?
options: A gilded statue of the Virgin Mary
A brass bell visible from the harbor
A weather vane shaped like a fishing boat

marseille-st-charles.text: Which French national anthem, 'La Marseillaise', was composed in Strasbourg in 1792 but is named after Marseille because of what event?
options: It was first performed publicly at Marseille's opera house
Marseille volunteers sang it as they marched to Paris
It was written by a Marseille-born poet while in Strasbourg
The French National Assembly voted to name it in Marseille's honor

marseille-st-charles.text: The 'Vieux-Port' (Old Port) of Marseille has served as the city's harbor for over 2,600 years. Roughly how many fishing boats can it accommodate today?
Marseille's football club Olympique de Marseille (OM) won the UEFA Champions League in 1993. Who was their legendary manager at the time?
What is the name of the rocky inlets (narrow creeks) along the coastline near Marseille that are popular with swimmers and hikers?
Which architect designed the Unité d'Habitation, a pioneering modernist apartment block completed in Marseille in 1952?
Marseille was designated European Capital of Culture in which year?
The MuCEM (Museum of European and Mediterranean Civilisations) opened in Marseille in 2013. What unusual feature connects it to the historic Fort Saint-Jean?
options: A subterranean tunnel under the Old Port
A suspended footbridge over the sea
A glass-enclosed aerial walkway at rooftop level

marseille-st-charles.text: Marseille is the second-largest city in France and also has Europe's largest which type of infrastructure?
The Panier district in Marseille is one of the city's oldest neighborhoods. What is it primarily known for today?
options: Its industrial warehouses converted into lofts
Its colorful streets, artisan shops, and street art
Its luxury hotels and Michelin-starred restaurants

marseille-st-charles.text: What is 'navette', the traditional biscuit of Marseille, shaped to resemble?
options: A fish, honoring the city's fishing heritage
A small boat or cradle, symbolizing the boat carrying saints to Provence
A cross, reflecting Marseille's Christian history
An olive leaf, celebrating Provençal agriculture

marseille-st-charles.text: Which major French writer, born in Aubagne near Marseille, is famous for his works about Provençal life including 'Jean de Florette'?
Marseille hosted matches during which major football tournament in 1998?
The 'savon de Marseille' (Marseille soap) is a traditional product with a protected designation. What percentage of olive oil must it contain to be authentic?
The Greek colony that founded Marseille was known by what ancient name?
Marseille has a large community with roots in North Africa, particularly Algeria. What name is given to French citizens of Algerian origin?
What is the name of the large park and former royal hunting ground on the outskirts of Marseille?

lille-europe.text: Lille is located in which historical region of northern France, close to the Belgian border?
The 'Braderie de Lille' is one of Europe's largest flea markets. When does it traditionally take place?
What is 'carbonnade flamande', a dish strongly associated with the Lille and Nord region?
options: A grilled pork sausage with mustard sauce
A slow-cooked beef stew made with beer
A fried fish dish with chips and tartare sauce
A rabbit braised in white wine and herbs

lille-europe.text: Lille was originally a Flemish city and was part of the Spanish Netherlands. In which year did France definitively take possession of the city?
The Palais des Beaux-Arts de Lille is considered one of the finest fine arts museums outside Paris. Which of these artists is NOT represented there?
The iconic Vieille Bourse (Old Stock Exchange) in Lille's Grand'Place is famous for what architectural style?
options: Gothic revival with pointed arches and flying buttresses
Baroque Flemish style with ornate gable facades
Classical French with a symmetrical colonnade
Art Nouveau with floral iron decorations

lille-europe.text: Lille was designated European Capital of Culture in which year?
Which French president and military leader was born in Lille in 1890?
What is the name of the metro system in Lille, notable for being among the world's first fully automated urban metro lines?
The local Lille football club, which won the Ligue 1 championship in 2011 and 2021, is known by what name?
What is 'chicorée', for which the Lille region is particularly well known?
options: A type of blue-glazed ceramic pottery
A coffee substitute made from roasted chicory root
A local dialect spoken in Nord-Pas-de-Calais
A regional beer brewed with bitter hops

lille-europe.text: Lille-Europe station, which opened in 1994, was built to serve which major new transport link?
options: The TGV line to Paris Montparnasse

lille-europe.text: The Euralille district of Lille was designed by which internationally renowned architect?
Which local beer style is traditionally associated with the Nord-Pas-de-Calais region around Lille?
The city of Lille has an alternative local name used by its Flemish-speaking neighbors. What is it?
The citadel of Lille, still used as a French military base, was designed by which famous French military engineer?
Lille is home to three universities. Which is the largest by student enrollment?
Which Flemish painter, born near Lille in 1577, is famous for his dramatic Baroque canvases and worked for King Philip IV of Spain?
What is the local Lillois slang term 'ch'ti' or 'chti' used to describe?
options: A person from Paris living in Lille
A native or resident of northern France (Nord-Pas-de-Calais)
The local beer brewed around Lille

lille-europe.text: The comedy film 'Bienvenue chez les Ch'tis' (2008) directed by Dany Boon, set in the Nord region around Lille, became the highest-grossing French film of all time at that point. Approximately how many French cinema tickets did it sell?

strasbourg.text: Strasbourg is the official seat of which major European institution?
The Strasbourg Cathedral, one of the tallest medieval buildings in the world, is famous for its intricate astronomical clock. In which century was the main body of the cathedral completed?
What is 'choucroute garnie', the iconic dish of Alsace and Strasbourg?
options: A cream-based cheese tart baked in a pastry shell
Fermented cabbage served with various meats and sausages
Roast duck with apple and sauerkraut stuffing
A potato gratin with Munster cheese and caraway

strasbourg.text: Strasbourg changed nationality between France and Germany several times. For how many years (approximately) was it part of the German Empire between 1871 and 1918?
Johannes Gutenberg, inventor of the movable-type printing press, lived and worked in Strasbourg for several years in the 1430s–40s. Which language name is the city known by in German?
The 'Grande Île' (Great Island) historic center of Strasbourg, surrounded by the Ill River, was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in which year?
Which traditional Alsatian pastry, a tall fluted cake baked in a distinctive mold, is particularly associated with Strasbourg and Alsace?
What is the 'Petite France' district in Strasbourg's old town known for?
options: Its collection of modern French art galleries
Its half-timbered houses along the canals of the Ill River
Its concentration of French-language bookshops and cafés
Its large open-air market selling French produce

strasbourg.text: The Council of Europe, distinct from the European Union, is also headquartered in Strasbourg. It is primarily known for which human rights instrument?
options: The UN Declaration of Human Rights
The European Convention on Human Rights

strasbourg.text: The Alsatian wine route stretches south of Strasbourg along which mountain range?
options: The Black Forest (on the German side)

strasbourg.text: Which grape variety, named after the region, is the signature white wine of Alsace and particularly associated with Strasbourg's wine culture?
The 'tarte flambée' (Flammekueche in Alsatian) is a pizza-like flatbread associated with Alsace. What are its classic toppings?
Strasbourg's Christmas market, 'Christkindelsmärik', is one of the oldest in Europe. Since approximately which century has it been held?
Which river forms the border between Strasbourg, France and Kehl, Germany?
The University of Strasbourg is one of France's largest universities. It merged back into a single institution in 2009. Which Nobel Prize winner was famously associated with Strasbourg's university?
options: Marie Curie, who studied chemistry there
Louis Pasteur, who served as dean of the science faculty
Albert Schweitzer, who studied theology and medicine there
René Cassin, who taught law there

strasbourg.text: The 'La Marseillaise' national anthem was composed in Strasbourg in 1792 by which officer?
What is the local Alsatian dialect language known as?
The Strasbourg tram network reopened in 1994 after decades without trams. It is now considered a model for European urban transport. How many lines does it have?
Which type of pâté, made from goose or duck liver and considered an Alsatian specialty, is heavily associated with the Strasbourg culinary tradition?
Which French writer, philosopher and satirist, famous for 'Candide', lived in Strasbourg and is said to have called the cathedral 'a sublime monster'?

bordeaux.text: Bordeaux is world-famous for its wine. Which of these is NOT one of the five 'First Growth' (Premier Grand Cru Classé) châteaux of the 1855 Bordeaux classification?
The Place de la Bourse in Bordeaux, built in the 18th century, is famous today for which modern addition in front of it?
options: The Miroir d'Eau (Water Mirror), the world's largest reflecting pool
A sunken amphitheater used for concerts
A large public fountain with Roman-style columns

bordeaux.text: Bordeaux was under English rule (as the capital of the Duchy of Aquitaine) for approximately how long during the Middle Ages?
The Cité du Vin, a wine-themed museum and cultural center in Bordeaux, opened in which year?
Which grape variety is used to make the majority of red Bordeaux wines, often in blends with Merlot?
The French philosopher Montesquieu, famous for his theory of separation of powers, was born near Bordeaux. His most famous work is which book?
options: The Discourse on the Origin of Inequality

bordeaux.text: Which major French river, formed by the confluence of the Garonne and the Dordogne, flows past Bordeaux and into the Atlantic?
Bordeaux's historic urban center was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2007. It is considered one of the finest examples of what architectural period?
The CAPC (Contemporary Art Museum) in Bordeaux is housed in what type of historic building?
The 'canelé' is a small pastry considered emblematic of Bordeaux. What is its characteristic texture?
options: Light and airy with a crisp outer shell, like a choux puff
Caramelized, dark, crispy crust with a soft, custardy interior
Dense, fudgy, and very chocolatey throughout
Flaky and buttery, like a miniature croissant

bordeaux.text: Which famous French essayist, known for inventing the essay form and writing 'Les Essais', was mayor of Bordeaux from 1581 to 1585?
The Bordeaux wine appellation on the right bank of the Gironde, known for Merlot-dominant wines, includes which famous village?
The Gate of Cailhau (Porte Cailhau) in Bordeaux was built as a triumphal arch in the late 15th century to celebrate which French military victory?
options: The expulsion of the English from Aquitaine in 1453
The victory at Fornovo by Charles VIII in 1495
The capture of Bordeaux from Burgundy in 1472

bordeaux.text: The annual wine festival 'Bordeaux Fête le Vin' takes place along which Bordeaux waterfront?
What is the name of the prestigious Bordeaux opera house, completed in 1780 and considered an architectural masterpiece?
Bordeaux is located in the Gironde department. What is the area to the west of the city, famous for pine forests, dunes, and Atlantic beaches?
Bordeaux's football club, Girondins de Bordeaux, plays in distinctive navy and white. They won how many French league titles (Ligue 1/Division 1)?
The TGV (high-speed rail) line from Paris to Bordeaux opened in 2017. Approximately how long does the journey now take?
Which famous Goya painting depicts the scene in Bordeaux during the Napoleonic Wars? (Goya spent his final years in exile in Bordeaux.)
options: The Shootings on Príncipe Pío Hill

bordeaux.text: The Bordeaux wine region is divided into sub-regions. Which sub-region, south of the city, produces famous sweet white wines including Sauternes?

nice.text: Nice is located in the Alpes-Maritimes department. It was part of which kingdom before being ceded to France in 1860?
options: The Kingdom of Sardinia (House of Savoy)

nice.text: Which Italian national hero was born in Nice in 1807 and is celebrated in Italy for unifying the country?
The famous seaside promenade of Nice is officially called what?
Nice is famous for its 'socca'. What is it?
options: A thin, crispy chickpea flour pancake
A pasta dish with tomato and basil
A pastry filled with anchovies and olives
A fried dough ball dusted with powdered sugar

nice.text: The Nice Carnival, held each February, is one of the world's largest carnivals. It culminates in what symbolic event?
options: A boat race in the Bay of Angels
The burning of the carnival king effigy at the sea
A grand fireworks display over the Promenade des Anglais
A costumed parade through the Old Town

nice.text: The painter Henri Matisse spent the latter part of his life in Nice. Which chapel did he design entirely, including stained glass, murals, and vestments, in nearby Vence?
What is 'salade niçoise', in its traditional Niçois form (as debated by purists)?
options: A warm salad of green beans, tomatoes, tuna, eggs, and anchovies — without any cooked vegetables except eggs
A cold pasta salad with olives, capers, and boiled potatoes
A mesclun salad topped with grilled tuna steak and hard-boiled eggs
A rice salad with peppers, olives, and shrimp

nice.text: The old town of Nice, known as 'Vieux-Nice', is famous for its distinctive architectural style. Which influence is most visible in its colorful baroque buildings?
Nice's bay is known by what evocative name, referring to its sweeping crescent shape?
The 2016 Bastille Day attack in Nice took place on the Promenade des Anglais. How many people were killed in this terrorist attack?
Which famous Russian Tsar helped make Nice fashionable among Russian aristocracy, and whose influence led to the construction of the Russian Orthodox Cathedral?
The Niçois dialect spoken historically in Nice is called 'Niçard' or 'Nissart'. To which language family does it belong?
options: A form of Ligurian (related to Genoese)
A mix of northern Italian and old Provençal

nice.text: Nice is the capital of which French department?
What is 'pissaladière', a dish strongly associated with Nice?
options: A pizza-like flatbread topped with caramelized onions, anchovies, and olives
A pasta dish with tomato sauce and cured meats
A vegetable gratin with goat cheese and herbs
A fish soup similar to bouillabaisse but thicker

nice.text: Nice's international airport, the third busiest in France, is named after which French aeronautics pioneer?
The Corniche roads above Nice offer spectacular views. How many Corniche roads run parallel along the coast between Nice and Monaco?
The flower market in Nice's Cours Saleya is considered one of the most beautiful in France. What else is the Cours Saleya also famous for selling?
options: Lavender products and essential oils, seasonally

nice.text: Nice's football club, OGC Nice, plays at the Allianz Riviera stadium. The club's nickname is 'Les Aiglons'. What does that mean?
The climate of Nice is classified as Mediterranean. On average, the city enjoys approximately how many sunny days per year?
The Baroque church of Saint-Jacques-le-Majeur (also called 'Gesù Church') in Old Nice was built in the 17th century. Which religious order built it?

toulouse-matabiau.text: Toulouse is nicknamed 'La Ville Rose' (The Pink City). What gives the city its distinctive rosy hue?
options: The red sandstone used for its buildings
The pink terracotta bricks used extensively in construction
The rose gardens planted throughout the city center
The pinkish granite quarried from the Pyrenees

toulouse-matabiau.text: Toulouse is the center of European aerospace manufacturing. Which major aircraft manufacturer has its headquarters there?
The Canal du Midi, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, begins near which city and connects it to the Mediterranean?
options: Toulouse, linking the Garonne and the Mediterranean

toulouse-matabiau.text: What is 'cassoulet', the dish most famous associated with the Toulouse region?
options: A light Provençal fish soup with rouille
A slow-cooked casserole of white beans, duck confit, and sausage
A potato gratin with duck fat and garlic
A spiced lamb stew with couscous

toulouse-matabiau.text: Which major medieval pilgrimage route passing through Toulouse was inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage list as part of the Routes of Santiago de Compostela?
options: Via Tolosana (Toulouse route, also called Via Arles)

toulouse-matabiau.text: The Basilica of Saint-Sernin in Toulouse is one of the largest Romanesque buildings in the world. It was built to accommodate what?
options: A large population of monks in a monastic complex
The large numbers of pilgrims on the way to Santiago de Compostela
The seat of the Archbishop of Toulouse and his clergy
A royal ceremonial church for the counts of Toulouse

toulouse-matabiau.text: Toulouse is home to France's largest university by student numbers. What is its approximate student population?
Which Toulouse-born or Toulouse-based mathematician gave his name to a famous theorem in number theory (his Last Theorem)?
Stade Toulousain (Toulouse rugby club) is the most successful club in European rugby history. The club is popularly known by what nickname?
options: Les Rouges et Noirs (Red and Blacks)

toulouse-matabiau.text: The Capitole de Toulouse is both the city hall and a theatre. What is particularly striking about its façade?
options: A tall Gothic spire visible from throughout the city
A grand 18th-century Classical facade opening onto a large square
A Baroque dome rivaling Sacré-Cœur in Paris
A modernist glass-and-steel structure by Jean Nouvel

toulouse-matabiau.text: The Albigensian Crusade of the 13th century was launched against the Cathar heresy. Toulouse was a center of Cathar sympathy. Who launched this crusade?
options: King Philip II of France (Philip Augustus)
Pope Innocent III, calling on northern French lords
The Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II
The King of Aragon, acting as protector of the Cathars

toulouse-matabiau.text: The Aeroscopia museum near Toulouse displays historic aircraft. Which supersonic passenger aircraft, jointly developed by France and Britain with Toulouse as its main production site, is prominently featured?
Which river flows through the center of Toulouse?
The Toulouse-Lautrec museum, holding the world's largest collection of Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec's work, is located not in Toulouse itself but in a nearby town. Which?
The 'violet of Toulouse' ('violette de Toulouse') is a celebrated local product. In what form is it most famously consumed?
options: As a herbal tea used in traditional medicine
Crystallized as candied violet sweets and in violet liqueur
As a fresh salad ingredient with olive oil
Distilled into a perfume exclusive to Toulouse

toulouse-matabiau.text: Which space agency, the European counterpart to NASA, has its main operations center in Toulouse?
The Place du Capitole, Toulouse's main square, is famous for an unusual pavement feature. What is embedded in its center?
options: A giant sundial using the city hall's shadow
A large cross of Languedoc (Occitan cross) in bronze
A mosaic map of the Canal du Midi
A fountain shaped like the Garuda, symbol of Toulouse

toulouse-matabiau.text: Toulouse is the capital of which French administrative region (since the 2016 regional reform)?
The ancient Roman city on the site of Toulouse was called what?
Which woad-derived blue dye, produced intensively in the Toulouse region during the 15th–16th centuries, brought enormous wealth to the local merchant class?

montpellier.text: The University of Montpellier, one of the oldest in the world, was founded in 1220 and is particularly renowned for what faculty?
The Place de la Comédie in Montpellier, the main city square, is commonly nicknamed what by locals?

Montpellier's historic city center contains no medieval walls or significant Roman ruins. This is because the city was founded relatively recently. In approximately which century was Montpellier established?
Montpellier is located close to which large coastal lagoon, famous for flamingos, bulls, and horses in the Camargue area?
François Rabelais, the Renaissance humanist writer famous for Gargantua and Pantagruel, studied medicine at Montpellier. In which century did he do so?
Montpellier was home to a large Arab and Jewish scholarly community in the Middle Ages. The city was at various times under the control of which Spanish kingdom before passing to France?
The Promenade du Peyrou in Montpellier, a 17th-century royal promenade, features an aqueduct at one end. What does the aqueduct bring to the city?
options: Fresh spring water from the Saint-Clément source
Gardon river water for the city's fountains
Hérault river water for the city's mills

montpellier.text: Which French king stayed in Montpellier while it was still subject to the Kingdom of Aragon and later acquired it for France in 1349?
options: Philip VI, who purchased it from the King of Majorca

montpellier.text: Montpellier's tram network, opened in 2000, is unusual for having trams decorated by famous artists and designers. Which designer created the first line's distinctive trams?
The Antigone district of Montpellier, a large neoclassical housing development, was designed by which architect?
Montpellier's football club, MHSC (Montpellier Hérault Sport Club), won the Ligue 1 title unexpectedly in which year?

The wine-producing area around Montpellier, in the Languedoc region, is the world's largest single wine-producing area. What wine style has traditionally dominated but is now being transformed with higher-quality production?
options: Fine white wines similar to Burgundy
Bulk red wine ('vin de table') now upgraded to AOC quality

montpellier.text: Montpellier is the capital of which French department?
The Zoo of Montpellier (Parc Zoologique de Montpellier) is notable for what unusual feature for a European city zoo?
options: It is Europe's largest zoo by area
It is completely free and open to the public
It was the first zoo in France to breed giant pandas

montpellier.text: Which prominent French sociologist and anthropologist, author of 'Distinction', was born in Montpellier?

The Fabre Museum in Montpellier holds one of the largest collections of fine art in France outside Paris. It is housed in a 17th-century hôtel particulier. Which painter gave it the bulk of its founding collection?
Montpellier has a large student population. Approximately what fraction of the city's inhabitants are students?
The Cathédrale Saint-Pierre in Montpellier has an unusual architectural feature: two large cylindrical towers flanking the entrance, reminiscent of a fortified castle. In which century was it built?
Montpellier is considered a 'young' city demographically. What is the main reason for this reputation?
options: High birth rates in the city's immigrant communities
A large student population combined with strong population growth by young residents
Government policy to attract young workers to tech industry
Proximity to beaches attracting young people seasonally

montpellier.text: The medical school of Montpellier still preserves a collection of human anatomical specimens in its historic building. Which famous Arab physician's works formed the basis of medical teaching there in the Middle Ages?

nantes.text: Nantes is historically associated with which famous royal edict, issued in 1598, granting religious freedom to French Protestants?
Nantes was for centuries a major port in the transatlantic slave trade. Approximately how many enslaved people were transported from Nantes-based ships, making it France's leading slave-trading port?
The Les Machines de l'Île attraction in Nantes, located on a former industrial island, is famous for which spectacular mechanical creation?
options: A giant mechanical elephant that carries passengers
A robotic recreation of Jules Verne's Nautilus submarine
A motorized reproduction of a 19th-century three-masted sailing ship

nantes.text: Which famous science fiction writer, author of 'Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea' and 'Around the World in Eighty Days', was born in Nantes in 1828?
The Château des Ducs de Bretagne in Nantes was the ducal seat of Brittany. In which room of this castle was Anne of Brittany born in 1477?
options: The Tour de la Couronne d'Or
The Duchess's bedchamber in the Grand Logis

nantes.text: Nantes is administratively part of which French region since 2016, even though historically it was the capital of Brittany?
The biscuit brand 'LU' (Lefèvre-Utile), famous for the Petit Beurre biscuit, was founded in Nantes. In which year was the iconic Petit Beurre created?
The 'Passage Pommeraye' in Nantes, opened in 1843, is considered one of the most beautiful examples of what type of urban structure?
options: A covered neoclassical shopping arcade on multiple levels
A 19th-century grand bazaar with Oriental motifs
A Haussmann-style galerie with apartments above shops

nantes.text: The Loire, the longest river in France, passes through Nantes before reaching the Atlantic. What is the name of the estuary it forms near Nantes?
options: The Loire Estuary (Estuaire de la Loire)

nantes.text: The FC Nantes football club is famous for which attacking style of play developed in the 1970s and 1980s?
options: Jeu à la nantaise (rapid passing and movement)

nantes.text: During the Revolutionary Terror in 1793–94, Nantes was the scene of mass executions of counter-revolutionary prisoners by drowning in the Loire. What were these called?
The Muscadet wine region, producing a crisp dry white wine popular with seafood, is centered around which city?
The Île de Nantes is an urban renewal project transforming the city's industrial island. The former shipyard cranes are now illuminated as a tourist landmark. What were the shipyards called?
options: Les Chantiers de l'Atlantique (Les Chantiers Dubigeon)

nantes.text: Anne of Brittany, twice Queen of France, married two French kings in succession. Who were they?
The local Nantaise specialty 'gâteau nantais' is a pastry made with which key alcohol ingredient?
Nantes hosts the 'Voyage à Nantes' art festival each summer, turning the city into an open-air exhibition. In which year was this festival created?
The writer and poet Jacques Prévert, famous for the poem 'Les Feuilles Mortes' (Autumn Leaves), was associated with which French artistic movement that had connections to Nantes?
Nantes' industrial heritage includes being home to one of France's first biscuit factories, built by the LU company. What architectural style is the famous LU tower (Tour LU)?
Which French artist, born in Nantes in 1900, was a pioneer of kinetic art and is particularly known for his motorized sculptures?
The area around Nantes and the Loire Estuary is home to the largest salt marsh producing 'fleur de sel' in France. What is this salt marsh area called?

rennes.text: Rennes is the capital of which French region?
In 1720, a great fire devastated most of medieval Rennes. This led to the city being rebuilt in a different architectural style. Which style dominates the rebuilt city center?
options: Gothic revival with slate-roofed timber frames

rennes.text: The Dreyfus Affair, one of France's most divisive political scandals, was dramatically impacted by a document forged in Rennes. But more relevantly, where was Dreyfus's retrial held in 1899?
What is the name of the large Saturday market held at the Place des Lices in Rennes, considered one of the largest in France?
options: Marché de la Place du Parlement

rennes.text: Rennes is famous for its traditional Breton crêpes and galettes. What is the key difference between a crêpe and a galette in Breton culinary tradition?
options: Crêpes are sweet and made with wheat flour; galettes are savoury and made with buckwheat flour
Crêpes are thick and fluffy; galettes are thin and crispy
Crêpes are served with butter; galettes are served with cream sauce
